one_line_takeaway: IntelGuard turns unstructured vendor threat-intel reports into a retrieval corpus of code-behavior-reasoning triples that an LLM consults via RAG, pushing PyPI/npm malicious-package detection to ~99% accuracy and cutting obfuscated-code false negatives from 880 to 28 relative to retrieval-free LLM baselines, while finding 54 unreported malicious PyPI packages (24 confirmed) in a live deployment.
key_finding: On PyPI (Table 1): IntelGuard achieves 99.00% accuracy / 99.49% precision / 98.50% recall / 98.99% F1, beating the best baseline MalGuard-SVM (94.75% accuracy) by 4.25 points; worst baseline Bandit4Mal scores 28.65% accuracy. On npm (Table 2): IntelGuard achieves 98.72% accuracy / 99.80% precision / 97.67% recall / 98.72% F1, beating best baseline MalPac-SVM (95.31%) and surpassing OSSGadget (56.39% -- stated in text as a 31.1-point gap, though the raw accuracy difference is closer to ~42 points) [text discrepancy noted]. On obfuscated PyPI (Table 3): IntelGuard maintains 96.50% accuracy (95.84% precision, 97.31% recall) vs. e.g. MalGuard-SVM dropping to 85.44% and SAP-RF dropping to 65.78% (a 23.17-point fall from its non-obfuscated 88.95%). Ablation (RQ4): IntelGuard (99.00% acc, 0.50% FPR) vs. direct GPT-4o-mini analysis without retrieval (96.99% acc, 2.11% FPR) on standard PyPI; on obfuscated PyPI, IntelGuard holds 96.50% accuracy while GPT-4o-mini collapses to 52.82% accuracy with 84.86% false-negative rate, and GPT-4o instead over-flags benign obfuscated code (62.12% FPR) -- a 43.68-point accuracy gap between IntelGuard and GPT-4o-mini attributed to retrieved expert reasoning. Retrieved knowledge reduces obfuscated-PyPI false negatives from 880 to 28 cases (paper calls this a '30x improvement'). Knowledge-base characterization (RQ3): of 7,889 reasoning chains, functional violation reasoning accounts for 4,579 cases (58%), contextual boundary violation 1,814 (23%), privilege abuse 946 (12%), temporal anomaly 550 (7%); of 14,076 violated-expectation statements, execution-context violations are 41.5%, functional-boundary 25.0%, permission-abuse 18.0%, data-flow 10.0%, isolation 5.5%. Extraction-quality validation on 300 human-audited entries: 95.7% inter-rater agreement on extraction faithfulness, 92.3% of violated-expectation statements correctly captured security principles, 89.6% of boundary distinctions were technically valid; LLM-vs-expert correlation Pearson r=0.91. Real-world deployment discovered 54 previously unreported malicious PyPI packages; 24 were officially confirmed/removed by PyPI administrators and the remaining 30 were independently removed before submission (verified malicious via OSV records).
core_contribution: IntelGuard, a retrieval-augmented-generation (RAG) framework for detecting malicious npm/PyPI packages that extracts structured 'expert reasoning' (code snippets + behavioral descriptions + causal explanations of why a behavior is malicious) from 8,024 threat-intelligence report pages (reduced via LLM filtering to 4,420 substantive pages, yielding 4,420 malicious code contexts / 7,889 reasoning chains) collected from 22 security vendors (e.g., ReversingLabs, Trend Micro, Kaspersky). At detection time, the target package is reduced to sensitive-API-centered code slices (via backward data-flow and control-flow slicing over static call graphs built with PyCG for Python and Jelly for JavaScript), each slice is embedded (dual code + behavior embeddings, alpha=beta=0.5 similarity weighting) and used to retrieve semantically similar malicious exemplars plus their expert reasoning chains from a hierarchically clustered (HDBSCAN, two-level) knowledge base; an LLM (GPT-4.1-mini for detection, GPT-4.1 for knowledge extraction, text-embedding-3-large for embeddings) then reasons over the slice + retrieved exemplars + reasoning chains to output a binary malicious/benign verdict with explanation. A package is flagged malicious if any slice is flagged.
threat_model: Software supply-chain attacks via malicious open-source packages published to npm/PyPI (pre-installation, static-analysis-based detection). Attack workflow modeled as: (1) publish malicious package, (2) deceive users into installing (typosquatting, dependency confusion, account compromise), (3) execute payload via sensitive APIs (network, encryption/Base64, process execution, file, system-info operations). The paper explicitly treats adversarial evasion of the detector itself as future work, not covered: 'although sensitive API slicing reduces attack surface, adversarial prompts targeting LLM-based detection remain a potential threat' (Section 6, Limitations). Obfuscation (variable renaming, control-flow flattening, string encoding via Intensio-Obfuscator) is evaluated as an evasion technique against detection accuracy, not as an attack on the LLM component per se.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Section 6): (1) knowledge-base validation relies primarily on LLM self-assessment, with only 300 of the (thousands of) extracted entries verified by human experts -- systematic bias may exist in the unvalidated majority despite the reported r=0.91 LLM-expert correlation. (2) The OCR pipeline used to recover code embedded as screenshots in vendor reports achieves only 87% accuracy; recognition errors in complex layouts can propagate into the knowledge base even after validation. (3) Sensitive-API slicing reduces attack surface but adversarial prompts targeting the LLM-based detector itself remain an acknowledged, unaddressed threat -- i.e., the system's own robustness to prompt injection/adversarial manipulation of the LLM reasoning stage is not evaluated. Additional grounding-relevant caveats not flagged by the authors as 'limitations' but visible in the text: the npm improvement-over-OSSGadget figure stated in prose ('31.1 percentage points') does not match the raw Table 2 numbers (98.72% - 56.39% = 42.33 points), an internal inconsistency in the paper's own reporting; the malicious test datasets are drawn from pre-existing published datasets (Ohm et al. for npm, the authors' own earlier Guo et al. PyPI dataset) rather than fully independent new corpora, and detection cost/latency ($0.0016/package, 5.3s) was only measured in the live deployment window, not benchmarked against baseline tool costs.

datasets_used: OSV (Open Source Vulnerabilities) database for seed list of confirmed malicious npm/PyPI packages

8,024 threat-intelligence report pages from 22 security vendors (e.g. ReversingLabs, Trend Micro, Kaspersky), filtered to 4,420 substantive pages / 4,420 malicious code contexts / 7,889 reasoning chains / 14,076 violated-expectation statements
PyPI test set: 1,000 malicious packages (from Guo et al. dataset) + 1,000 benign (top-5,000-download PyPI packages)
npm test set: 1,000 malicious packages (from Ohm et al. dataset) + 1,000 benign (top-5,000-download npm packages)
Obfuscated PyPI test set: 1,041 malicious + 1,019 benign, transformed with Intensio-Obfuscator (variable renaming, control-flow flattening, string encoding)
Live PyPI.org deployment corpus, July 8 - October 1, 2025
quantitative_results: PyPI: IntelGuard 99.00% accuracy / 99.49% precision / 98.50% recall / 98.99% F1 (Table 1)
npm: IntelGuard 98.72% accuracy / 99.80% precision / 97.67% recall / 98.72% F1 (Table 2)
Obfuscated PyPI: IntelGuard 96.50% accuracy / 95.84% precision / 97.31% recall / 96.57% F1 (Table 3), a 2.5-point drop from non-obfuscated PyPI

Ablation vs. direct GPT-4o-mini (no retrieval): 99.00% vs 96.99% accuracy (standard PyPI, 0.50% vs 2.11% FPR); 96.50% vs 52.82% accuracy on obfuscated PyPI (GPT-4o-mini FNR 84.86%); GPT-4o instead shows 62.12% FPR on obfuscated benign code
Obfuscated-PyPI false negatives: 880 (GPT-4o-mini baseline) reduced to 28 (IntelGuard), described as ~30x improvement
npm false negatives reduced from 44 to 24 cases vs. comparison point, with lower FPR (0.20% vs 2.30%)

Knowledge-extraction validation: 95.7% inter-rater agreement; 92.3% of violated-expectation statements correctly captured security principles; 89.6% of boundary distinctions technically valid; LLM-expert Pearson r=0.91 (300 human-audited entries)
Reasoning-chain taxonomy (n=7,889): functional violation 58% (4,579), contextual boundary violation 23% (1,814), privilege abuse 12% (946), temporal anomaly 7% (550)
Violation-type taxonomy (n=14,076): execution-context 41.5%, functional-boundary 25.0%, permission-abuse 18.0%, data-flow 10.0%, isolation 5.5%
Live deployment: 54 previously unreported malicious packages found; 24 officially confirmed by PyPI security team, 30 independently removed pre-submission; cost $0.0016/package, ~5.3s/package
baselines_compared: PyPI baselines: Bandit4Mal, GuardDog, OSSGadget, PyPIWarehouse, SocketAI, SAP-DT, SAP-RF, SAP-XGB, MalGuard-DT, MalGuard-MLP, MalGuard-NB, MalGuard-RF, MalGuard-SVM, MalGuard-XGB
npm baselines: Genie, GuardDog, MalPac-MLP, MalPac-NB, MalPac-SVM, OSSGadget, Packj_static, Packj_trace, SAP-DT, SAP-RF, SAP-XGB, SocketAI
Ablation baselines: direct LLM analysis without retrieved knowledge using GPT-4o-mini and GPT-4o
